predictions.model_note: Forward predictions are warning-index forecasts, not probabilities. They use Open-Meteo daily forecast values combined with the same historical baseline and terrain context used by the current report.

resources.explanation: Local resource cache adds hydrology, water bodies, urban/impervious proxies, vegetation/forest, and agriculture context. These features refine relative zone exposure; they are not official hazard maps.

zone_analysis.explanation: Zone values are relative exposure indices, not probabilities and not official warning polygons. They are anchored to the overall AOI hazard level, then adjusted by local terrain. A higher zone value means this sector is more exposed than other monitored sectors if that hazard materializes.
zone_analysis.method: Named Iasi AOI sectors using elevation samples, OSM hydrology/land-cover context, recent weather, historical baseline factors, and global AOI risk indices

drought.explanation: Drought risk is 49.0% (medium) because expected precipitation is 26.0 mm/24h, observed mean temperature over the last 24 hours is 28.4 C, and soil moisture is 31.0% of the wet reference. For drought, precipitation below 5.0 mm/24h and temperatures above 30.0 C increase risk. The score now uses previous years for the same seasonal window, so one dry forecast day is not enough by itself to create high drought risk. Current temperature is 27.6 C. In this calculation: precipitation reduces risk, temperature strongly increases risk, dry soil moderately increases risk, 30-day historical dryness moderately increases risk.

normal_limits.high: near-zero precipitation, dry soil, and temperature above 30.0 C
normal_limits.low: above 5.0 mm/24h or sufficiently moist soil
normal_limits.medium: very low precipitation and temperature rising toward 30.0 C

drought.title: Drought

flood.explanation: Flood risk is 33.8% (low) because the forecast shows 26.0 mm of precipitation in the next 24 hours, then adjusts that signal using recent wetness versus same-season historical rainfall and terrain. The high-risk reference threshold is 50.0 mm/24h; values below 20.0 mm/24h are normally treated as low risk.

normal_limits.high: above 35.0 mm/24h, critical reference at 50.0 mm/24h
normal_limits.low: below 20.0 mm/24h

flood.title: Flood

heatwave.explanation: Heatwave risk is 52.5% (medium) because the observed maximum temperature over the last 24 hours is 34.2 C. The forecast maximum for the next 24 hours is 34.2 C. Risk starts increasing above 30 C, and the high-risk reference threshold is 38.0 C.

normal_limits.high: near/above 38.0 C
normal_limits.low: below 30 C
normal_limits.medium: approximately 30-35.0 C

heatwave.title: Heatwave

storm.explanation: Storm risk is 53.1% (medium) because maximum forecast gusts are 14.5 m/s and CAPE is 1100 J/kg. The high-risk wind reference is 25.0 m/s; CAPE above 2500 J/kg indicates strong convective instability.

normal_limits.high: gusts above 17.5 m/s, critical reference 25.0 m/s, or very high CAPE
normal_limits.low: gusts below 10.0 m/s and low CAPE
normal_limits.medium: gusts between 10.0 and 17.5 m/s or moderate CAPE

storm.title: Storm / strong wind

wildfire.explanation: Spontaneous wildfire risk index is 41.9% (medium). This is not a probability; it is a normalized warning index. The model now requires dry fuel conditions plus ignition weather, then uses wind mainly as a spread amplifier. It combines historical dryness, observed 24-hour maximum temperature 34.2 C, and wind gusts 14.5 m/s. Risk rises sharply above 32.0 C and above 12.0 m/s. Historical dryness is compared with previous years for the same season, so wind alone or one dry forecast day should not produce an excessive spontaneous-fire score. Current temperature is 27.6 C.

normal_limits.high: temperature above 32.0 C, wind above 12.0 m/s, and dry soil
normal_limits.low: temperatures below 25 C, weak wind, and moist soil
normal_limits.medium: rising temperature, wind near 12.0 m/s, or drought signs

wildfire.title: Wildfire

notes: elevated risks: drought, storm, heatwave, wildfire

resources.explanation: Local resource cache adds hydrology, water bodies, urban/impervious proxies, vegetation/forest, and agriculture context. These features refine relative zone exposure; they are not official hazard maps.

wildfire.explanation: Spontaneous wildfire risk index is 50.9% (medium). This is not a probability; it is a normalized warning index. predictions.model_note: Forward predictions are warning-index forecasts, not probabilities. They use Open-Meteo daily forecast values combined with the same historical baseline and terrain context used by the current report.

resources.explanation: Local resource cache adds hydrology, water bodies, urban/impervious proxies, vegetation/forest, and agriculture context. These features refine relative zone exposure; they are not official hazard maps.

zone_analysis.explanation: Zone values are relative exposure indices, not probabilities and not official warning polygons. They are anchored to the overall AOI hazard level, then adjusted by local terrain. A higher zone value means this sector is more exposed than other monitored sectors if that hazard materializes.
zone_analysis.method: Named Iasi AOI sectors using elevation samples, OSM hydrology/land-cover context, recent weather, historical baseline factors, and global AOI risk indices

drought.explanation: Drought risk is 49.0% (medium) because expected precipitation is 26.0 mm/24h, observed mean temperature over the last 24 hours is 28.4 C, and soil moisture is 31.0% of the wet reference. For drought, precipitation below 5.0 mm/24h and temperatures above 30.0 C increase risk. The score now uses previous years for the same seasonal window, so one dry forecast day is not enough by itself to create high drought risk. Current temperature is 27.6 C. In this calculation: precipitation reduces risk, temperature strongly increases risk, dry soil moderately increases risk, 30-day historical dryness moderately increases risk.

normal_limits.high: near-zero precipitation, dry soil, and temperature above 30.0 C
normal_limits.low: above 5.0 mm/24h or sufficiently moist soil
normal_limits.medium: very low precipitation and temperature rising toward 30.0 C

drought.title: Drought

flood.explanation: Flood risk is 33.8% (low) because the forecast shows 26.0 mm of precipitation in the next 24 hours, then adjusts that signal using recent wetness versus same-season historical rainfall and terrain. The high-risk reference threshold is 50.0 mm/24h; values below 20.0 mm/24h are normally treated as low risk.

normal_limits.high: above 35.0 mm/24h, critical reference at 50.0 mm/24h
normal_limits.low: below 20.0 mm/24h

flood.title: Flood

heatwave.explanation: Heatwave risk is 52.5% (medium) because the observed maximum temperature over the last 24 hours is 34.2 C. The forecast maximum for the next 24 hours is 34.2 C. Risk starts increasing above 30 C, and the high-risk reference threshold is 38.0 C.

normal_limits.high: near/above 38.0 C
normal_limits.low: below 30 C

predictions.model_note: Forward predictions are warning-index forecasts, not probabilities. They use Open-Meteo daily forecast values combined with the same historical baseline and terrain context used by the current report.

resources.explanation: Local resource cache adds hydrology, water bodies, urban/impervious proxies, vegetation/forest, and agriculture context. These features refine relative zone exposure; they are not official hazard maps.

zone_analysis.explanation: Zone values are relative exposure indices, not probabilities and not official warning polygons. They are anchored to the overall AOI hazard level, then adjusted by local terrain. A higher zone value means this sector is more exposed than other monitored sectors if that hazard materializes.
zone_analysis.method: Named Iasi AOI sectors using elevation samples, OSM hydrology/land-cover context, recent weather, historical baseline factors, and global AOI risk indices

predictions.model_note: Forward predictions are warning-index forecasts, not probabilities. They use Open-Meteo daily forecast values combined with the same historical baseline and terrain context used by the current report.

resources.explanation: Local resource cache adds hydrology, water bodies, urban/impervious proxies, vegetation/forest, and agriculture context. These features refine relative zone exposure; they are not official hazard maps.

zone_analysis.explanation: Zone values are relative exposure indices, not probabilities and not official warning polygons. They are anchored to the overall AOI hazard level, then adjusted by local terrain. A higher zone value means this sector is more exposed than other monitored sectors if that hazard materializes.
zone_analysis.method: Named Iasi AOI sectors using elevation samples, OSM hydrology/land-cover context, recent weather, historical baseline factors, and global AOI risk indices

drought.explanation: Drought risk is 36.8% (low) because expected precipitation is 0.0 mm/24h, observed mean temperature over the last 24 hours is 15.5 C, and soil moisture is 27.9% of the wet reference. For drought, precipitation below 5.0 mm/24h and temperatures above 30.0 C increase risk. The score now uses previous years for the same seasonal window, so one dry forecast day is not enough by itself to create high drought risk. Current temperature is 21.6 C. In this calculation: precipitation strongly increases risk, temperature reduces risk, dry soil strongly increases risk, 30-day historical dryness reduces risk.

normal_limits.high: near-zero precipitation, dry soil, and temperature above 30.0 C
normal_limits.low: above 5.0 mm/24h or sufficiently moist soil
normal_limits.medium: very low precipitation and temperature rising toward 30.0 C

drought.title: Drought

flood.explanation: Flood risk is 14.8% (low) because the forecast shows 0.0 mm of precipitation in the next 24 hours, then adjusts that signal using recent wetness versus same-season historical rainfall and terrain. The high-risk reference threshold is 50.0 mm/24h; values below 20.0 mm/24h are normally treated as low risk.

normal_limits.high: above 35.0 mm/24h, critical reference at 50.0 mm/24h
normal_limits.low: below 20.0 mm/24h

flood.title: Flood

heatwave.explanation: Heatwave risk is 3.6% (low) because the observed maximum temperature over the last 24 hours is 21.6 C. The forecast maximum for the next 24 hours is 25.3 C. Risk starts increasing above 30 C, and the high-risk reference threshold is 38.0 C.

normal_limits.high: near/above 38.0 C
normal_limits.low: below 30 C
normal_limits.medium: approximately 30-35.0 C

heatwave.title: Heatwave

storm.explanation: Storm risk is 37.4% (low) because maximum forecast gusts are 14.4 m/s and CAPE is 0 J/kg. The high-risk wind reference is 25.0 m/s; CAPE above 2500 J/kg indicates strong convective instability.

normal_limits.high: gusts above 17.5 m/s, critical reference 25.0 m/s, or very high CAPE
normal_limits.low: gusts below 10.0 m/s and low CAPE
normal_limits.medium: gusts between 10.0 and 17.5 m/s or moderate CAPE

storm.title: Storm / strong wind

wildfire.explanation: Spontaneous wildfire risk index is 24.5% (low). This is not a probability; it is a normalized warning index. The model now requires dry fuel conditions plus ignition weather, then uses wind mainly as a spread amplifier. It combines historical dryness, observed 24-hour maximum temperature 21.6 C, and wind gusts 14.4 m/s. Risk rises sharply above 32.0 C and above 12.0 m/s. Historical dryness is compared with previous years for the same season, so wind alone or one dry forecast day should not produce an excessive spontaneous-fire score. Current temperature is 21.6 C.

normal_limits.high: temperature above 32.0 C, wind above 12.0 m/s, and dry soil
normal_limits.low: temperatures below 25 C, weak wind, and moist soil
normal_limits.medium: rising temperature, wind near 12.0 m/s, or drought signs

wildfire.title: Wildfire

elevation.explanation: Elevation context: center is about 154.0 m, sampled range is 57.0-631.0 m, terrain class is steep. Steeper terrain can increase runoff speed and local flood response; flatter terrain can hold standing water longer.

drought.explanation: Drought risk is 29.4% (low) because expected precipitation is 0.0 mm/24h, observed mean temperature over the last 24 hours is 15.2 C, and soil moisture is unknown. For drought, precipitation below 5.0 mm/24h and temperatures above 30.0 C increase risk. The score now uses previous years for the same seasonal window, so one dry forecast day is not enough by itself to create high drought risk. In this calculation: precipitation strongly increases risk, temperature reduces risk, 30-day historical dryness reduces risk.

wildfire.explanation: Spontaneous wildfire risk index is 6.9% (low). This is not a probability; it is a normalized warning index. The model now requires dry fuel conditions plus ignition weather, then uses wind mainly as a spread amplifier. It combines historical dryness, observed 24-hour maximum temperature 21.7 C, and wind gusts 8.5 m/s. Risk rises sharply above 32.0 C and above 12.0 m/s. Historical dryness is compared with previous years for the same season, so wind alone or one dry forecast day should not produce an excessive spontaneous-fire score.

normal_limits.high: temperature above 32.0 C, wind above 12.0 m/s, and dry soil
normal_limits.low: temperatures below 25 C, weak wind, and moist soil
normal_limits.medium: rising temperature, wind near 12.0 m/s, or drought signs

predictions.model_note: Forward predictions are warning-index forecasts, not probabilities. They use Open-Meteo daily forecast values combined with the same historical baseline and terrain context used by the current report.

resources.explanation: Local resource cache adds hydrology, water bodies, urban/impervious proxies, vegetation/forest, and agriculture context. These features refine relative zone exposure; they are not official hazard maps.

zone_analysis.explanation: Zone values are relative exposure indices, not probabilities and not official warning polygons. They are anchored to the overall AOI hazard level, then adjusted by local terrain. A higher zone value means this sector is more exposed than other monitored sectors if that hazard materializes.
zone_analysis.method: Named Iasi AOI sectors using elevation samples, OSM hydrology/land-cover context, recent weather, historical baseline factors, and global AOI risk indices

drought.explanation: Drought risk is 36.6% (low) because expected precipitation is 3.1 mm/24h, observed mean temperature over the last 24 hours is 24.1 C, and soil moisture is 33.4% of the wet reference. For drought, precipitation below 5.0 mm/24h and temperatures above 30.0 C increase risk. The score now uses previous years for the same seasonal window, so one dry forecast day is not enough by itself to create high drought risk. Current temperature is 32.0 C. In this calculation: precipitation reduces risk, temperature moderately increases risk, dry soil moderately increases risk, 30-day historical dryness reduces risk.

normal_limits.high: near-zero precipitation, dry soil, and temperature above 30.0 C
normal_limits.low: above 5.0 mm/24h or sufficiently moist soil
normal_limits.medium: very low precipitation and temperature rising toward 30.0 C

drought.title: Drought

flood.explanation: Flood risk is 14.6% (low) because the forecast shows 3.1 mm of precipitation in the next 24 hours, then adjusts that signal using recent wetness versus same-season historical rainfall and terrain. The high-risk reference threshold is 50.0 mm/24h; values below 20.0 mm/24h are normally treated as low risk.

normal_limits.high: above 35.0 mm/24h, critical reference at 50.0 mm/24h
normal_limits.low: below 20.0 mm/24h

flood.title: Flood

heatwave.explanation: Heatwave risk is 28.6% (low) because the observed maximum temperature over the last 24 hours is 32.0 C. The forecast maximum for the next 24 hours is 34.5 C. Risk starts increasing above 30 C, and the high-risk reference threshold is 38.0 C.

normal_limits.high: near/above 38.0 C
normal_limits.low: below 30 C
normal_limits.medium: approximately 30-35.0 C

heatwave.title: Heatwave

storm.explanation: Storm risk is 46.0% (medium) because maximum forecast gusts are 17.7 m/s and CAPE is 0 J/kg. The high-risk wind reference is 25.0 m/s; CAPE above 2500 J/kg indicates strong convective instability.

normal_limits.high: gusts above 17.5 m/s, critical reference 25.0 m/s, or very high CAPE
normal_limits.low: gusts below 10.0 m/s and low CAPE
normal_limits.medium: gusts between 10.0 and 17.5 m/s or moderate CAPE

storm.title: Storm / strong wind

wildfire.explanation: Spontaneous wildfire risk index is 40.1% (medium). This is not a probability; it is a normalized warning index. The model now requires dry fuel conditions plus ignition weather, then uses wind mainly as a spread amplifier. It combines historical dryness, observed 24-hour maximum temperature 32.0 C, and wind gusts 17.7 m/s. Risk rises sharply above 32.0 C and above 12.0 m/s. Historical dryness is compared with previous years for the same season, so wind alone or one dry forecast day should not produce an excessive spontaneous-fire score. Current temperature is 32.0 C.

normal_limits.high: temperature above 32.0 C, wind above 12.0 m/s, and dry soil
normal_limits.low: temperatures below 25 C, weak wind, and moist soil
normal_limits.medium: rising temperature, wind near 12.0 m/s, or drought signs

wildfire.title: Wildfire

storm.title: Storm / strong wind

wildfire.explanation: Spontaneous wildfire risk index is 20.4% (low). This is not a probability; it is a normalized warning index. The model now requires dry fuel conditions plus ignition weather, then uses wind mainly as a spread amplifier. It combines historical dryness, observed 24-hour maximum temperature 32.0 C, and wind gusts 17.7 m/s. Risk rises sharply above 32.0 C and above 12.0 m/s. Historical dryness is compared with previous years for the same season, so wind alone or one dry forecast day should not produce an excessive spontaneous-fire score.

predictions.model_note: Forward predictions are warning-index forecasts, not probabilities. They use Open-Meteo daily forecast values combined with the same historical baseline and terrain context used by the current report.

resources.explanation: Local resource cache adds hydrology, water bodies, urban/impervious proxies, vegetation/forest, and agriculture context. These features refine relative zone exposure; they are not official hazard maps.

zone_analysis.explanation: Zone values are relative exposure indices, not probabilities and not official warning polygons. They are anchored to the overall AOI hazard level, then adjusted by local terrain. A higher zone value means this sector is more exposed than other monitored sectors if that hazard materializes.
zone_analysis.method: Named Iasi AOI sectors using elevation samples, OSM hydrology/land-cover context, recent weather, historical baseline factors, and global AOI risk indices

normal_limits.high: near-zero precipitation, dry soil, and temperature above 30.0 C
normal_limits.low: above 5.0 mm/24h or sufficiently moist soil
normal_limits.medium: very low precipitation and temperature rising toward 30.0 C

storm.title: Storm / strong wind

wildfire.explanation: Spontaneous wildfire risk index is 6.6% (low). This is not a probability; it is a normalized warning index. The model now requires dry fuel conditions plus ignition weather, then uses wind mainly as a spread amplifier. It combines historical dryness, observed 24-hour maximum temperature 18.6 C, and wind gusts 8.0 m/s. Risk rises sharply above 32.0 C and above 12.0 m/s. Historical dryness is compared with previous years for the same season, so wind alone or one dry forecast day should not produce an excessive spontaneous-fire score.

normal_limits.high: temperature above 32.0 C, wind above 12.0 m/s, and dry soil
normal_limits.low: temperatures below 25 C, weak wind, and moist soil
normal_limits.medium: rising temperature, wind near 12.0 m/s, or drought signs

predictions.model_note: Forward predictions are warning-index forecasts, not probabilities. They use Open-Meteo daily forecast values combined with the same historical baseline and terrain context used by the current report.

resources.explanation: Local resource cache adds hydrology, water bodies, urban/impervious proxies, vegetation/forest, and agriculture context. These features refine relative zone exposure; they are not official hazard maps.

zone_analysis.explanation: Zone values are relative exposure indices, not probabilities and not official warning polygons. They are anchored to the overall AOI hazard level, then adjusted by local terrain. A higher zone value means this sector is more exposed than other monitored sectors if that hazard materializes.
zone_analysis.method: Named Iasi AOI sectors using elevation samples, OSM hydrology/land-cover context, recent weather, historical baseline factors, and global AOI risk indices
